title-group.article-title: Functional Recovery After Pulmonary Embolism: A Pilot Study Using Metabolic Monitoring During the 6-Minute Walk Test

article-meta.aff: 1Division of Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine, University of Rochester Medical Center, Rochester, NY, United States

fn.p: Contributorship: Substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work (DL, DR, RJW); the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work (DL, DR, KN, DH, RJW); Drafting the work or reviewing it critically for important intellectual content (DL, DR, KN, DH, RJW); Final approval of the version to be published (DL, DR, KN, DH, RJW); Agreement to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved (DL, DR, KN, DH, RJW). All authors have read and approved submission of the manuscript and the manuscript has not been published and is not being considered for publication elsewhere in whole or part in any language except as an abstract. All authors meet the 4 ICMJE criteria for authorship.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Daniel Lachant, 601 Elmwood Ave, Box 692, Rochester, NY, 14620, Daniel_Lachant@urmc.rochester.edu, Fax: 585-486-0947

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Disability after acute pulmonary embolism is common. Functional testing is not routinely performed in early follow-up and typically occurs only when symptoms persist after three months of anticoagulation.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This pilot study was a single center, prospective, observational study as part of a larger rehabilitation clinical trial. We recruited patients hospitalized for intermediate or high risk acute pulmonary embolism. Baseline (within 2 weeks of discharge) and follow up (3 months later) testing included 6-minute walk test with metabolic monitoring, Cardiac Effort (number of heart beats used/walk distance), two weeks of actigraphy, and PE-mb Quality of Life Questionnaire. Non-parametric and mixed method repeated measures regression were performed.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Twenty-five participants completed paired six-minute walk testing after acute PE. No safety concerns emerged when performing the walk test. There was significant improvement in walk distance, +58 m (29, 108), and Cardiac Effort, −0.2 beats/m (−0.4, −0.1), at 3-month follow up. Metabolic monitoring showed improvement in oxygen consumption, +1.8 ml/kg/min (0.8, 4.9), and respiratory efficiency, −2.9 (−7.7, −0.5). Actigraphy showed increased moderate activity time, +10 min/day (4, 21), and quality of life improved, −20% (−24, −7). Both 6-minute walk distance and actigraphy parameters were associated with metabolic parameters.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: For a group of participants that were asymptomatic at 3 months after hospitalization for acute pulmonary embolism, early functional assessments that can be obtained and repeated in an office setting demonstrated recovery in function and physiology (gas exchange and heart rate). Early testing could help better characterize recovery after acute pulmonary embolism.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Functional impairment and reduced quality of life after acute pulmonary embolism (PE) are commonly reported (1–3). These sequelae are frequently described as Post-PE Impairment(2) or Post-PE Syndrome(4) with estimates ranging from 16% (2) to 50%(3, 5) depending on the patient population and criteria used. The largest study to date used a combination of echocardiographic findings and functional assessment to define Post-PE Impairment(2); however, there remains no consensus definition regarding which symptoms or physiologic abnormalities can be directly attributed to the acute PE or what constitutes recovery. Easily obtained functional measures paired with physiologic monitoring may offer a more practical and scalable approach characterizing recovery and identifying possible contributors to persistent symptoms after acute PE.

Multiple mechanisms have been proposed to explain ongoing dyspnea following PE, including deconditioning, anxiety, and both recognized and unrecognized comorbidities, while also acknowledging important but less common causes such as chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) and chronic thromboembolic pulmonary disease (CTEPD) without pulmonary hypertension (3, 6–9). After three months of anticoagulation, dyspnea is often evaluated with stationary cycle cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) (1, 10, 11). CPET abnormalities typically show deconditioning (1, 10, 11) or ventilatory inefficiency (increased minute ventilation [VE] / volume of carbon dioxide [VCO2]) (12). Despite its ease, 6-minute walk distance (6MWD) is not commonly reported or included in the evaluation of recovery after PE or Post-PE Impairment. Similarly, no prior studies have reported actigraphy-derived measures of real-world physical activity, even though these data could objectively quantify patient-perceived activity limitations.

As part of a pilot home-based rehabilitation program after acute PE (13), we administered a quality of life questionnaire and measured functional parameters in the clinic and at home after acute PE. We obtained continuous heart rate and portable metabolic data using the Cosmed K5 system(14) during a standardized 6-minute walk test (6MWT) (15) to characterize cardiac and pulmonary physiology during self-paced walking. Home activity was captured through wrist-worn ActiGraph GTX9, and quality of life was assessed using PE-mb Quality of life questionnaire (PE-mb QoL) (16). In this exploratory analysis, the primary objectives were to evaluate metabolic parameters early after hospital discharge, assess whether changes could be detected during recovery after acute PE at 3 month follow up, and examine how these metabolic changes relate to other assessments, including actigraphy and quality of life measures. We hypothesized that: 1) during 6MWT the volume of oxygen (VO2) would be reduced, ventilatory efficiency (VE/VCO2) would be elevated, and Cardiac Effort (a surrogate for stroke volume, number of heart beats/walk distance)(17, 18) would be increased; 2) VO2, VE/VCO2, and Cardiac Effort would improve at 3 month follow up; and 3) VO2 and VE/VCO2 during 6MTW would be associated with PE-mb QoL questionnaire(16), and actigraphy (Steps and moderate activity time).

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: This was a single-center, prospective, and exploratory sub-study with IRB approval and reported on clinicaltrials.gov (NCT 05571189). As part of the 12-week rehabilitation study, participants either completed an unsupervised, low-intensity, heart rate–guided exercise program or underwent heart rate monitoring alone. Exercise intensity was prescribed to remain below 75% of the age-predicted maximum heart rate. We recruited patients managed by the pulmonary embolism response team (PERT) for intermediate or high-risk PE(19) between December 2022 and March 2024. We excluded those who could not ambulate (e.g., orthopedic trauma) or had an estimated survival <12 months (e.g., metastatic cancer) from the parent study (13); for this sub-study, we also excluded those with a supplemental oxygen requirement or significant facial hair that precluded secure mask fitting (for the Cosmed metabolic monitoring device). We did not exclude any pulmonary or cardiovascular comorbidities, and these were common as typical for a hospitalized, intermediate risk PE population (13). Baseline testing occurred within 14 days of hospital discharge and again 3 months later, including PE-mb QoL questionnaire (16) and wrist worn ActiGraph GTX9 on the non-dominant wrist for 14 days. Participants performed 6MWT according to American Thoracic Society (ATS) guidelines(15) wearing Cosmed K5(14) (calibrated and worn as recommended by the manufacture) and Fourth Frontier X2 continuous heart rate monitor (chest strap). For actigraphy, only days with >10-hour wear time during waking hours were included for analysis. Repeat testing was completed after 3 months alongside clinical assessment by a physician from our Pulmonary Hypertension Association-accredited center to evaluate for Post-PE Impairment. Standard of care echocardiography is part of our post-PERT assessment at 3 months(3). Baseline demographics were extracted from the electronic health records. Heart failure preserved ejection fraction and obstructive lung disease were documented if the diagnosis was present in the record at the time of acute PE.

Metabolic parameters measured with the Cosmed K5 during standardized 6MWT included respiratory quotient (RQ), volume of oxygen (VO2) consumption, VO2 adjusted by weight, volume of carbon dioxide (VCO2), minute ventilation (VE), and ventilatory efficiency (VE/VCO2), and estimated dead space (Vd)/tidal volume (Vt). We recorded both peak and values averaged during the 6MWT. We measured heart rate with the Fourth Frontier X2, a single lead electrocardiogram obtained from a dry electrode worn as a chest strap. Heart rate was averaged every 4 seconds and number of heart beats during 6MW was used to calculate Cardiac Effort (# of heart beats during 6MW/6MW distance). We also recorded resting heart rate, peak heart rate, heart rate expenditure (total heart beats used during 6MW), heart rate at 6 and 7 minutes, heart rate reserve (peak heart rate – resting heart rate), and heart rate recovery (heart rate at 6 minutes – 7 minutes). As metabolic testing during the 6MWT has not been extensively reported, a group of healthy volunteers (not age/sex matched) also completed the 6MWT with Cosmed K5 and Fourth Frontier. For actigraphy, we recorded total daily steps, light activity time, and moderate activity time. We calculated daily values and then averaged them during each 14-day monitoring period.

sec.title: STATISTICS
sec.p: Continuous variables are reported as median with IQR. Categorical variables are reported as count and percentage. Paired and non-parametric testing were performed for group comparison. We used mixed-effects repeated-measures regression to evaluate associations between functional testing metrics and quality-of-life scores with metabolic parameters. Age, baseline body mass index, and acute PE treatment modality were included as fixed effects, and a random intercept was specified at the patient level. Given the small sample size, imputations were not performed for missing data. R 4.3.0 and GraphPad Prim 9 were used for analysis.

sec.title: DEMOGRAPHICS
sec.p: Out of 32 participants, 29 consented to baseline measurements but only 25 had paired measurements to be included in this analysis. Two people wanted to participate but could not wear Cosmed K5 because of facial hair and another declined participating in the sub-study. Four additional participants did not have follow up measurements due to changes in their visit schedule such that the Cosmed K5 could not be calibrated and used during the second assessment. They recovered at follow-up visit and were not otherwise different than those for whom we report data. Baseline demographics of the 25 participants and the healthy reference cohort are listed in Table 1. PE participants were older, obese males. All participants experienced intermediate risk PE, and all were treated with anticoagulation. In participants requiring advanced therapy, anticoagulation was given first. The median hospital stay was 3 days. On discharge all participants were on a direct oral anticoagulant. At 3-month follow-up, none of the 25 participants were classified as having Post-PE Impairment. Echocardiogram showed recovery in right ventricle size and function in all participants.

sec.title: 6-MINUTE WALK DISTANCE AND CARDIAC EFFORT
sec.p: The median 6MWD at baseline was 412 m and increased by +58 m at 3-month follow up (Table 2). Borg Dyspnea Index, resting heart rate, peak heart rate, heart rate at 6-minutes, heart rate at 7-minutes, heart rate reserve, or heart rate recovery did not differ between visits. Cardiac Effort, which indexes the number of heart beats for the 6MWD, decreased significantly (−0.2 beats/m) at follow up (Table 2). The small healthy cohort group, completing their first 6MWT, provides physiologic context for these values.

sec.title: METABOLIC PARAMETERS
sec.p: Peak and average VO2 increased significantly at 3-month follow-up (Table 2). There was also a significant decrease in VE/VCO2 (improved ventilatory efficiency) and calculated Vd/Vt at follow up (using an estimated PaCO2). Respiratory quotient increased at follow up, suggesting greater effort during 6MWT.

sec.title: ANTICOAGULATION COMPARED TO PERCUTANEOUS INTERVENTION
sec.p: Among the 25 participants with paired data, 19 were treated with anticoagulation alone and 6 were treated with large bore mechanical thrombectomy or thrombolytics and anticoagulation (Table 3). During baseline assessment within 14 days of discharge (the majority of testing occurred within 7 days), gas exchange parameters did not differ between treatment groups. 6MWD was better in the anticoagulation group for this small sample.

sec.title: ACTIGRAPHY AND QUALITY OF LIFE
sec.p: There was high compliance with ActiGraph wear in all 25 subjects (Table 4). There was a trend for increase in average daily steps during recovery after PE. Total activity time increased at 3-month follow up, primarily driven by change in moderate activity. PE-mb QoL score decreased 20% at follow up (improved quality of life).

sec.title: METABOLIC PARAMETERS CORRELATE WITH FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY OF LIFE
sec.p: Across all mixed-effects models, 6MWD was the most consistent and strongest predictor of metabolic performance (Supplement Table 1). Greater 6MWD was associated with higher peak and average VO2 (both absolute and weight-normalized) and with lower VE/VCO2. These patterns indicate that longer 6MWD corresponds to better cardiopulmonary metabolic responses during exertion. A higher Cardiac Effort was associated with lower peak and average VO2 and higher VE/VCO2. Elevated PEmb-QoL score aligned with impaired VO2 and elevated VE/VCO2. Higher moderate activity time was associated with a lower VE/VCO2.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Our findings demonstrate that incorporating heart rate and metabolic monitoring during 6MWT is a feasible and safe way to obtain meaningful physiologic information early after acute PE. Even within two weeks of discharge, participants were able to complete standardized hallway 6MWT without physician supervision. We observed no safety concerns and acceptance was high; there was only one participant who declined to wear the Cosmed K5. Improvements were seen in VO2, VE/VCO2, respiratory quotient, a stroke volume surrogate (Cardiac Effort (17, 18)), actigraphy, and patient-reported quality of life during the recovery after acute PE. Our data suggest that these relatively simple measurements capture clinically relevant functional and ventilatory performance for those who self-identify as “recovered” and provides insight into capacity of activity versus what people actually do in the home setting after acute PE.

Post-PE Impairment is a broad and heterogeneous term that includes rare causes such as CTEPH as well as more common contributors like deconditioning, anxiety, and underlying cardiopulmonary comorbidities (6–9, 20). Especially since older age (2, 20, 21), obesity (20, 21), and smoking history(20, 21) are associated with impairment after acute PE, and more so than clot burden on index imaging (20) or RV dysfunction on follow-up echocardiography(2), distinguishing which symptoms reflect persistent physiologic limitation versus chronic medical conditions is challenging. Current guideline-recommended follow-up relies heavily on imaging and, in select cases, CPET (7). Specifically, Post–PE Impairment can occur with or without residual perfusion defects or vascular obstruction on imaging at least 3 months after acute PE (5). Conversely, approximately half of patients with residual perfusion defects do not report persistent symptoms. Therefore, in the absence of symptoms, imaging alone may be of limited clinical utility and is less helpful with recovery. Our results suggest that a 6MWT with additional equipment can provide relevant physiologic data, particularly heart rate changes and ventilatory efficiency, that might inform clinical decisions at a very early time after hospital discharge (e.g., unsupervised exercise training or the ability to return to work). Similarly, comparing discharge testing to that at early followup might uncover physiologic limitations that could be associated with symptoms. In patients who cannot undergo metabolic monitoring because of supplemental oxygen or facial hair, Cardiac Effort can still provide insight into residual stroke volume limitation (i.e., exaggerated heart rate response for a given walk distance). We believe an early 6MWT incorporating physiologic monitoring is an easy way to capture recovery in multiple metabolic and heart rate variables. In patients with abnormal physiologic monitoring or with minimal recovery at follow up, earlier targeted evaluation with imaging, CPET, rehabilitation, or invasive testing for CTEPH could be employed.

Actigraphy also appears promising as a complimentary measurement for contextualizing patient-reported symptoms. We are currently unaware of actigraphy being used to monitor recovery after acute PE. Although none of our participants had any symptoms of Post-PE Impairment, their overall activity remained low, and actigraphy provided objective evidence of improvement in moderate activity levels over three months. Moderate activity time, which requires more cardiopulmonary fitness, was associated with respiratory efficiency, whereas daily steps is influenced less by physiology and more by behavior(22). Incorporating passive monitoring could help clinicians recognize inactivity-related deconditioning, reinforce the value of exercise, and differentiate physiologic limitation from behavioral or comorbidity-related contributors to dyspnea.

Most interventional clinical studies in acute PE have not focused on changes in 6MWD (23–29). In ELOPE, 6MWD increased significantly between 1 and 3 months (21). In a sub-group of FOCUS, the median 6MWD was 450 m in n=313 participants at 3 month follow up (30). This group had significant baseline comorbidities and 32% had either immobility, trauma, or cancer prior to their PE, which could have influenced 6MWD. 6MWD did not change between 3 and 12 months in patients with Post-PE Impairment.

Previous investigators have assessed CPET during recovery after PE. CPET testing in ELOPE showed reduced VO2 (<80% predicted) at 1 month, likely related to deconditioning (21). In a smaller cohort of submassive and massive PE patients (11), the VO2 achieved at 1 month on cycle ergometer was similar to values we measured during 6MWT (within 14 days). In FOCUS, CPET testing showed 50% had a circulatory or respiratory limitation (10). Importantly, the FOCUS trial noted the challenges of using CPET testing in assessment of Post PE Impairment, specifically that testing is limited to patients able (or willing) to perform a maximal effort. In a predominantly lower risk PE cohort, CPET was safely performed within 1 month of hospital discharge (31), and that study highlights the growing enthusiasm for obtaining physiologic assessment early after acute PE. 6MWT with telemetry and metabolic monitoring could provide clinicians and researchers with a less expensive, more readily available, and more patient accepted assessment.

This study has limitations, this is a small, single-center sample size. None of the participants developed Post-PE Impairment or CTEPH; thus, we don’t know what absolute or follow up metabolic parameters obtained during 6MWT would suggest Post-PE Impairment or CTEPH. We are currently enrolling a larger, multi-year study and hope to report data from a subgroup of symptomatic patients (i.e., Post-PE Impairment). This current data was collected as part of a randomized home exercise study, and instruction about the value of activity and other aspects of the parent study likely contributed to some improvement observed. We found remarkably high patient engagement but lost paired measurement in 4 subjects because of last minute schedule changes in location where the 6MWT was performed (COVID restrictions) that did not allow for Cosmed to be calibrated. While we do not have a full CPET to compare to the Cosmed-measured values during 6MWT, the portable Cosmed has been compared to a complete metabolic system (Vyntus) previously(14). In that study of healthy people, they found VO2, VCO2, VE/VCO2, and RER measured by Cosmed were within <5% of Vyntus at 60 W exercise intensity. Future studies should compare 6MWT with Cosmed to a full CPET in a non-healthy cohort, especially in a PE cohort for whom CPET is often the test of choice. Quantitative echocardiographic parameters such as tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) and right ventricular fractional area change (RVFAC) were not routinely reported and could not be compared to baseline testing. Symptom onset was unable to be reliably collected in all participants and could be compared to metabolic testing.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: In this exploratory study, submaximal exercise testing with metabolic and heart rate monitoring may represent a feasible, safe and novel assessment of patients during PE recovery. It could help clinicians identify patients at risk for Post PE impairment at a very early timepoint based on abnormal ventilatory or heart rate response during 6MWT. As structured follow up programs are now guideline recommended after acute PE(32) to help identify CTEPH(33), these functional assessments can be obtained within the first week of hospital discharge. This early data could help define physiologic limitations after acute PE and also provide a baseline to judge recovery at a later time. As 30-day clinical outcomes for intermediate-risk PE continue to improve, we propose that early, readily available functional assessments could be used to evaluate efficacy for therapeutic response. Further studies are needed to evaluate whether early functional assessments might be a simple way to identify those who need early CTEPH evaluation, as shown to be helpful in this large Sheffield cohort(33), or be new clinical trial outcomes for acute PE.

ack.title: Acknowledgement:
ack.p: We want to thank all the patients and families who participated in this study.

sec.title: Funding:
sec.p: The project described in this publication was supported in part by University of Rochester CTSA award No. KL2 TR001999 (to D. L.) from the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences of the National Institutes of Health, Chest Foundation Grant (to D.L.), and NHLBI 1K23HL171867-01 (to D.L.). Authors Roto, Haight, Norton, and White had no funding.

fn.p: Conflict of interest: None declared (DL, DR, KN, DH, RJW) related to this research.
Ethical approval: IRB approval from the University of Rochester was obtained.
During the preparation of this work the author(s) did not use any AI tool/services.
Guarantor: DL is the guarantor of the data.

td.bold: Low Molecular Weight Heparin + Suction Thrombectomy, %
Low Molecular Weight Heparin + Systemic Thrombolytics, %

fn.p: N-terminal prohormone of brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP). Median with interquartile range. Controls are provided as a reference value.

caption.p: Changes in Heart Rate and Metabolic Parameters during 6-Minute Walk Test.

fn.p: Minute Ventilation (VE), Volume Carbon Dioxide (CO2), Dead Space (VD), Tidal Volume (Vt), Arterial Blood Gas (ABG). Median with interquartile range. Controls are provided as a reference value only. Paired non-parametric testing was performed with Wilcoxon ranked test.

caption.p: Baseline comparison between acute pulmonary embolism patients treated with anticoagulation and advanced intervention.

fn.p: Minute Ventilation (VE), Volume Carbon Dioxide (CO2), Dead Space (VD), Tidal Volume (Vt), Arterial Blood Gas (ABG). Median with interquartile range. Nonparametric testing was performed with Mann-Whitney test.

caption.p: Changes in actigraphy during acute PE recovery.

fn.p: Median and Interquartile range. Paired non-parametric testing was performed with Wilcoxon ranked test.
